title: Leverage Regulation and Market Structure: A Structural Model of the U.K. Mortgage Market
abstract: I develop a structural model of mortgage demand and lender competition to study how leverage regulation affects the U.K. mortgage market. Using variation in risk-weighted capital requirements across lenders and mortgages with different loan-to-values (LTVs), I show that a 1-percentage-point increase in risk-weighted capital requirements increases lenders' marginal cost of originating mortgages by about 26 basis points (11%) on average. I use the estimated model to study proposed leverage regulations. Counterfactual analyses show that large lenders exploit a regulatory cost advantage, which increases concentration by about 20%, and suggest that banning high-LTV mortgages may reduce large lenders' equity buffer.

article.introduction:
As a result of the globalfinancial crisis, several European countries and the United States introducednew regulations to prevent excessive bank leverage and increase the stability of the financial system. Prominent examples include risk-weighted minimum capital requirements for lenders and limits to loan-to-income (LTI) and loan-to-value (LTV) for borrowers (Acharya, Engle, and Pierret (2014), Behn, Haselmann, and Wachtel (2016a), Jiménez et al. (2017), DeFusco, Johnson, and Mondragon (2020)). While there is relatively wide agreement on the necessity of such measures, the design of lender- and borrower-based leverage regulations and their implications for the allocation of credit have been subject to debate.
To better link capital to risk, policymakers have allowed lenders to invest in internal rating-based (IRB) models to calculate the risk weights on their activities. IRB models may improve risk management practices by financial intermediaries, but they are costly to develop and maintain. As a result, a two-tier system has emerged whereby large lenders adopt IRB models, while small lenders use the standardized regulatory approach. This heterogeneity across lenders and asset classes can have unintended consequences, such as potential regulatory arbitrage and reduced competition (Acharya, Schnabl, and Suarez (2013), Behn, Haselmann, and Vig (2016b), Greenwood et al. (2017), Buchak et al. (2018b)).1
In this paper, I develop a structural model to quantify the cost of risk-weighted capital requirements and the equilibrium impact of heterogeneous leverage regulations on interest rates, concentration, and risk in the mortgage market, which played a central role in the global financial crisis (Mian and Sufi (2011), Campbell and Cocco (2015), Corbae and Quintin (2015)). To capture the richness in product differentiation, households' choices, and lenders' capital requirements in modern mortgage markets, I take an approach inspired by the industrial organization literature on differentiated product demand. Specifically, I estimate my model using loan-level data on the universe of mortgage originations in the United Kingdom and a novel identification strategy that exploits variation in leverage regulation both across lenders and across mortgages with different LTVs.2
I begin by providing descriptive evidence that large lenders adopting IRB models have lower risk weights than small lenders across almost all mortgages they originate. I also show that the difference in risk weights is larger for mortgages with lower leverage and cannot be explained by observable ex-ante borrower characteristics or ex-post mortgage performance. As a result, large lenders have an absolute advantage across all mortgages they originate and a relative advantage across mortgages with lower leverage, all else equal. I label this phenomenon the regulatory-risk-weight gap.
I next explore how heterogeneity in risk-weight regulation affects banks' ability to fund themselves cheaply, which is their most important competitive edge (Hanson, Kashyap, and Stein (2011)). First, I show that the large differences in regulatory risk weights affect lenders' pricing behavior and their specialization patterns in different LTV segments of the mortgage market. Second, I exploit variation from the approval of an IRB model for a medium-size lender in an event-study setting to study the effect of changes in risk weights on mortgage prices.3
Overall, my descriptive evidence suggests that leverage regulations affect lenders' pricing and mortgage originations, consistent with recent work in other countries and markets (Behn, Haselmann, and Vig (2016b), Gropp et al. (2019)). However, understanding the equilibrium impact of leverage regulations using a purely reduced-form approach faces the challenge of isolatingobservedchanges in regulations from contemporaneousunobservedchanges in market power and business strategies, which can interact with regulatory changes and confound their effects. Furthermore, regulatory changes are endogenous, and different regulations are often adjusted simultaneously, making identification more complex (Kovner and Van Tassel (2018)).
Motivated by the descriptive evidence, I develop a workhorse structural model that allows me to recover borrowers' demand elasticities and lenders' unobservable costs of originating mortgages. On the demand side, I assume that borrowers have heterogeneous preferences over interest rates and other characteristics (rate type, lender, and maximum leverage) and choose the mortgage product that gives them the highest utility across their individual-specific menu. I depart from traditional individual-level discrete-only choice models and allow borrowers to also choose the loan amount, following Dubin and McFadden (1984). The discrete-continuous choice allows me to decompose the elasticity of demand to the interest rate into a product elasticity and a loan demand elasticity, both affecting substitution patterns and lenders' markups.
On the supply side, I model lenders as heterogeneous multiproduct firms that offer differentiated mortgages and compete on interest rates, subject to regulatory leverage constraints. I use the elasticity parameters from the demand estimation together with lenders' Lerner formula for profit-maximizing prices and loan-level data on arrears to back out unobservable marginal costs of originating mortgage products.
I estimate the model using the universe of mortgage originations in the United Kingdom over the period 2015 to 2016. The United Kingdom represents an ideal laboratory to estimate my model given the rich variation in mortgage products and risk-weighted capital requirements. I estimate the demand-side parameters using variation in product characteristics and borrower choices. I address endogeneity problems arising from the simultaneous nature of the discrete-continuous choice and from omitted variables by instrumenting endogenous rates using predetermined risk-weighted capital requirements as cost shifters.
I estimate the supply-side parameters using variation in product characteristics that affect marginal costs via default risk and interest rate risk. Notably, I exploit variation in risk-weighted capital requirements both across lenders and across leverage levels within lender to identify the shadow cost of capital regulation. My empirical strategy therefore controls for differences across lenders that are common among products (lender shocks) and differences across products that are common across lenders (market shocks).
The estimated model captures several salient features of the data, such as market shares across lenders and LTVs, and performs well in predicting these features out of sample using additional data for 2017. On the demand side, I find an average loan demand elasticity of about 0.07 and a product demand elasticity of 5.9 across all mortgage products. These results straddle estimates from previous work based on different approaches (DeFusco and Paciorek (2017), Fuster and Zafar (2021)), as well as estimates from related work using structural approaches (Buchak et al. (2018a), Robles-Garcia (2019)).
On the supply side, I find an average markup of around 50 basis points, which corresponds to about 18% of the average interest rate in the data, and an average marginal cost of about 2.4%. Marginal costs are on average greater for mortgage products with higher LTV bands (capturing default risk) and a longer initial fixed period (capturing interest rate risk), which is intuitive. More importantly, I find that a 1-percentage-point higher risk-weighted capital requirement increases the average mortgage product marginal cost by 11% and interest rate by 10% (26 basis points).
Next, I use the estimated structural model to investigate the equilibrium effects of counterfactual leverage regulations in the mortgage market. Holding the estimated demand and marginal cost parameters fixed, I simulate different leverage regulations, allowing lenders and borrowers to best respond to the new regulations. These counterfactual simulations allow me to explore the design and equilibrium effects of alternative policies that are not observed in the data on mortgage rates, market structure, and risk.
In a first set of counterfactuals, I study the effects of removing the regulatory-risk-weight gap between large banks and other competitors. This analysis is motivated by recent proposals to reform capital requirements (Basel Committee on Banking Supervision (2016a,2016b)). Most notably, I simulate a counterfactual in which all lenders calculate risk-weighted capital requirements using the standardized approach and an alternative counterfactual in which small and medium lenders also adopt a representative IRB model.
Imposing a common standardized approach to risk weights increases the equity buffer of larger lenders as well as their costs, which are passed on to borrowers with large changes in market shares and decline in both lender profits and consumer surplus. The provision of a representative IRB model to small lenders addresses the competitive distortion due to the differential regulatory treatment, but with limited impact to consumer surplus, lenders profits, ex-post mortgage defaults, and the resilience of largest lenders. Overall, removing the policy-driven heterogeneity in risk weights reduces market concentration by approximately 20%. This procompetitive effect decreases the intermediation of the largest lenders, which played a central role in the crisis, and reduces their systemic importance.
Finally, I use the estimated model to explore possible interactions between capital requirements and limits to household leverage that have recently been discussed and implemented in some countries (Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (2013), Bank of England (2014), Acharya et al. (2020), DeFusco, Johnson, and Mondragon (2020)). I introduce a maximum LTV limit that removes from borrowers' menu all mortgages with leverage larger than 90%, both in an economy with risk-weighted capital requirements and in a counterfactual economy with homogeneous capital requirements (as was the case before the financial crisis under Basel I).
I find that a regulation targeting high LTV can reduce borrower defaults, which decline by 3% to 5%, but has a negative impact on originations and consumer surplus, as first-time buyers value mortgages with high leverage. Most notably, I show that the interactions of regulations on borrowers and lenders can have distributional effects and unintended consequences. Abolishing high-LTV mortgages has larger effects on consumer surplus and lender profits under homogeneous capital requirements, when low-LTV borrowers are “subsidizing” high-LTV borrowers, while the opposite is true under risk-weighted capital requirements. Finally, I show that introducing leverage limits in a market with risk-weighted capital regulation reduces large lenders' equity buffer and as a result increases systemic risk. This finding highlights the importance of looking at equilibrium effects, especially when multiple regulations are in place and the implications of changing a particular regulation may depend on other policies.
Related literature. This paper contributes to several strands of literature. First, I provide a new framework to study households' mortgage demand, which complements existing approaches in household finance using life-cycle portfolio choice models (Campbell and Cocco (2003), Campbell (2013)) or clever empirical identification strategies (DeFusco and Paciorek (2017), Best et al. (2020), Fuster and Zafar (2021)).4Specifically, my structural approach uses state-of-the-art techniques from the industrial organization literature on differentiated product demand systems and on discrete-continuous choice models (Lancaster (1979), Dubin and McFadden (1984), Berry, Levinsohn, and Pakes (1995), Hendel (1999), Nevo (2001), Thomassen et al. (2017)). The characteristics approach captures rich heterogeneity in household preferences and product availability along several dimensions, which are otherwise difficult to model together. The discrete-continuous approach allows me to decompose the impact of interest rates on households' joint choice of lender, leverage, and loan size. Within the household finance literature, my paper is one of the first to also account for lenders' response to demand preferences using a structural equilibrium model.
Second, my work is related to a growing literature that employs structural techniques to quantitatively study the industrial organization of financial markets. Recent papers look at retail deposits (Egan, Hortaçsu, and Matvos (2017), Xiao (2020)), insurance (Koijen and Yogo (2016)), corporate lending (Crawford, Pavanini, and Schivardi (2018)), pensions (Hastings, Hortaçsu, and Syverson (2017)), credit cards (Nelson (2019)), and mortgages (Guiso et al. (2017), Buchak et al. (2018a,2018b)). My paper is one of the first to apply similar techniques to the mortgage market and study the implications of leverage regulation for consumers and market structure. Most notably, while previous studies focus largely on a “representative” product for each lender and only model the choice across lenders, I exploit more granular variation in risk weights across asset classeswithinlenders to identify the elasticity of both product and loan demand and the impact of leverage regulation. In this sense, my paper is closest to Buchak et al. (2018a), who study several regulations in the United States using a structural industrial organization model that allows for rich heterogeneity in borrowers' product and quantity choices and lenders' financing options. My work complements their insights on supply-side factors that affect the equilibrium in the mortgage market by looking at the U.K. context, where banks keep most mortgages on their balance sheets and government interventions are less ubiquitous.5
Third, this paper contributes to the literature on the costs and benefits of capital regulations (Freixas and Rochet (2008), Admati and Hellwig (2014), Vives (2016), Greenwood et al. (2017)). Motivated by the global financial crisis, several papers have exploited different sources of variation to quantify the effects of capital requirements on the cost of capital and credit supply (Kisin and Manela (2016), Kovner and Van Tassel (2018), Gropp et al. (2019)); and to explore possible unintended consequences, such as risk taking (Acharya and Steffen (2015)), procyclicality (Behn, Haselmann, and Wachtel (2016a), Jiménez et al. (2017)), and regulatory arbitrage (Acharya, Schnabl, and Suarez (2013), Acharya, Engle, and Pierret (2014), Behn, Haselmann, and Vig (2016b), Plosser and Santos (2018)). On the empirical side, I provide new descriptive evidence of how risk-weighted regulation affects mortgage rates, with implications for lenders' competitiveness and specialization in different risk segments.
My work is most related to recent papers studying the effects of risk-weighted capital requirements on credit supply in the corporate loan market. For example, Gropp et al. (2019) find that banks exposed to higher capital requirements reduced their credit supply of syndicated loans relative to unaffected banks; and Behn, Haselmann, and Vig (2016b) show that the introduction of model-based regulation in Germany led to lower risk weights and underprediction of defaults but higher interest rates and loan losses for large banks relative to smaller banks not adopting the model-based approach. I complement these papers by focusing on the mortgage market and combining a structural model with an identification strategy that addresses the challenge of separately identifying demand- and supply-side factors in a context in which multiple banking relationships are rare. Most notably, the structural approach allows me to evaluate equilibrium trade-offs of counterfactual policies, which can inform the policy debate when several alternatives are under consideration and experimentation can be costly.
My work also speaks to an alternative set of borrower-based macroprudential policies that have been implemented in several countries since the global financial crisis (Hanson, Kashyap, and Stein (2011), Cerutti, Claessens, and Laeven (2017)). Among others, DeFusco, Johnson, and Mondragon (2020) show that the Dodd-Frank “Ability-to-Repay” rule in the United States reduced household leverage, by increasing the cost of originating high-leverage mortgages, but with little effect on defaults, while Acharya et al. (2020) find that the introduction of LTI and LTV limits in Ireland led to reallocation across households with limited aggregate effects. Similar to these studies, I provide evidence on the effects of regulation on borrowers leverage, studying with administrative-level data a policy in the United Kingdom that limits the fraction of high-LTI mortgages that banks can originate. Different from previous studies, I develop a structural model that allows for rich heterogeneity across households and exploits detailed microdata for estimation. Most importantly, the model allows me to explore how borrower-based macroprudential policies interact with lender-based macroprudential policies, thus providing new insights on complex equilibrium interactions in the presence of multiple regulations.
Finally, my work is related to the recent macrofinance literature that has developed quantitative general equilibrium models of mortgage markets with heterogenous agents (Greenwald (2018), Corbae and D'Erasmo (2019), Justiniano, Primiceri, and Tambalotti (2019), Begenau and Landvoigt (2021)). I adopt a partial equilibrium approach and focus mainly on the cost side of leverage regulation, which prevents me from implementing a fully fledged cost-benefit analysis of alternative regulations. Nevertheless, I explicitly model the interaction between leverage regulation and the competitive environment and the resulting implications for the pass-through of capital requirements to mortgage rates. In this sense, my work is also related to recent studies emphasizing the role of lenders' market power for the transmission of policy interventions (Scharfstein and Sunderam (2016), Agarwal et al. (2017), Drechsler, Savov, and Schnabl (2017)). Most importantly, I show that market structure is an endogenous outcome of leverage regulations that affect policy transmission to the real economy. This equilibrium interaction between leverage regulation and market structure has important implications for the design of policies to promote both stability and competition in the banking industry.
Overview. The remainder of the paper is organized as follows. SectionIdescribes the institutional setting and data sources. SectionIIprovides motivating facts on the U.K. mortgage market. SectionIIIdevelops the demand and supply model. SectionIVdescribes the estimation approach and the identification strategy. SectionVdiscusses the main results. SectionVIpresents counterfactual analyses. SectionVIIconcludes.
article.I. Institutional Setting and Data:
While mortgages are extremely important in all countries, mortgage systems vary widely across countries (Campbell (2013)). Three key institutional features differentiate the U.K. mortgage market from mortgage markets in the United States, Canada, and other countries in continental Europe.
First, in contrast for example to the U.S. mortgage market, long-term fixed-rate mortgages are unavailable in the United Kingdom (Badarinza, Campbell, and Ramadorai (2017), Cloyne et al. (2019)). The typical mortgage contract is characterized by a 25-year amortization period but a relatively low interest rate for a short initial period, which is followed by a penalizing reset rate. The initial rate typically has a duration of two to five years and may be either fixed or floating. During the initial period, most contracts have large early repayment charges, typically 5% or 10% of the outstanding loan, which makes refinancing before the end of the initial period costly.6After the end of the initial period, the mortgage rate resets to a standard variable rate (SVR). The SVR is always a floating rate and it is paid until either the borrower refinances the loan or maturity. Given the high prepayment penalty and the large jump in the reset rate, most borrowers in the U.K. refinance around the time when the initial period ends, which leads remortgaging to be a relatively frequent event in this market (Cloyne et al. (2019), Belgibayeva et al. (2020)).
Second, unlike other countries such as the United States and Canada, in the United Kingdom, there is limited consumer-based pricing or negotiation between the borrower and the lender (Allen, Clark, and Houde (2014), Buchak et al. (2018a)). As a result, the advertised rate is close to the rate that the borrower pays.7Figure1shows a snapshot from a popular search platform for mortgages in the United Kingdom after filling in information on the value of the property and the loan amount. The key mortgage characteristics are the loan provider, the type of interest rate, and the maximum LTV (e.g., a mortgage offered by Barclays with a two-year fixed rate and a maximum 90% LTV). I define a product as a lender, interest rate type, and maximum LTV triplet and I regress loan-level interest rates on product fixed effects and an extensive set of additional controls.
Panel (A) of FigureIA.1in theInternet Appendixshows that my product definition alone captures more than 70% of the variation in the loan-level rate. TheR2reaches 85% when I interact the product dummies with time dummies, and reaches more than 90% when I also include origination fee dummies. Adding dummies for the location of the house and borrower-level controls (age, income, house value, joint application, employment status) does not explain the residual variation in the rate. While I do not have access to credit score data and therefore cannot explicitly control for the effect of borrower credit scores on mortgage rates, Robles-Garcia (2019) shows that they do not seem to matter for mortgage pricing in the United Kingdom after conditioning for the relevant product characteristics.8This also differentiates the United Kingdom from the United States, where credit scores together with LTV bands affect mortgage rates.9
Third, the industrial organization of the mortgage market in the United Kingdom has some distinctive features. In particular, the U.K. banking market has become concentrated as a result of mergers and acquisitions over the last 40 years, particularly after the global financial crisis. As a result, in 2015, the six largest lenders accounted for more than 75% of new mortgage originations.10In more recent years, competition has increased with the entry of new challenger banks and with the introduction of regulatory interventions, such as forced divestment, but the market remains concentrated by international standards.11Thus, the industrial organization of the mortgage market in the United Kingdom is more similar to that of countries like Canada, where a few large banks account for the majority of mortgage originations, than to the United States (Allen, Clark, and Houde (2014), Scharfstein and Sunderam (2016)).
My paper combines several sources of administrative data. The main data set is the Product Sales Database (PSD) on residential mortgage originations collected by the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA). These data include the universe of residential mortgage originations by regulated entities since 2005.12I observe the main contract characteristics of the loan (rate type, repayment type, initial period, interest rate, lender), the borrowers (income, age), and the property (value, location, size). For the structural estimation, I focus on the years 2015 and 2016, during which all lenders report all contract characteristics that I employ in the analysis.
I complement the information on mortgage originations with four additional data sets. First, I use an additional source collected by the FCA that contains information from lenders' balance sheets on the performance of outstanding mortgages as of June 2016. Second, I exploit data on lenders' capital requirement and resources from the historical regulatory databases held by the Bank of England together with additional information from a survey of all lenders adopting IRB models in the United Kingdom on risk weights applied to mortgages by LTV band.13Third, for all lenders in my sample, I collect postcode-level data on their branches in the United Kingdom in 2015 from SNL financial. Fourth, I match borrowers' house with the house price index at the postcode level from the Office for National Statistics (ONS) database.
Panel A of TableIreports summary statistics for the universe of mortgages originated in the United Kingdom in 2015 and 2016 with an LTV above 50%.14In Panel A, I show the main data set on mortgage originations. I observe more than 1 million mortgage contracts, with an average rate of about 2.7 percentage points and an origination fee of £600. Mortgages fixed for two and five years together account for more than 85% of all originations. The average loan value is about £170,000, with an LTV of 77% and an LTI of 3.3. The sample is balanced across first-time buyers, home movers, and remortgagers. The average maturity is 25 years, and the average borrower is 35 years old with an income of around £57,000.
In Panel B of TableI, I report summary statistics on lenders' capital requirements, risk weights, and branches. The capital requirements include both minimum requirements under Basel II (Pillar I, or 8% of risk-weighted assets), as well as lender-specific supervisory add-ons (Pillar II). Total capital resources include all classes of regulatory capital, including common equity Tier 1, additional Tier 1, and Tier 2. The average capital divided by total risk-weighted assets is 17% when I focus only on Tier 1 and 21% when I include all classes of regulatory capital. The average capital requirement is 12%, ranging from a minimum requirement of 8% to a maximum of 22%, including all add-ons. The average risk weight is 17% and there is a lot of variation across lenders, leverage levels, and over time: the standard deviation is 11% and risk weights vary from a minimum of less than 1% to a maximum of almost 70%. The average number of lenders' branches in a postcode area is about seven, ranging from a minimum of 1 to a maximum of 63.
Finally, Panel C of TableIreports statistics on mortgage performance as of June 2016. On average 1.3% of outstanding mortgages are in arrears, which is defined as 90 or more days delinquent on monthly payments. Almost 85% of mortgages are not paying the SVR, which, on average, is around 3.7%.
article.II. Motivating Facts:
The U.K. mortgage market has several institutional features that make it an excellent laboratory for investigating the relationship between leverage regulation, mortgage pricing, and market structure. In this section, I provide a brief overview of four empirical facts that guide my assumptions underlying the structural model of SectionIII, and I describe some of the main sources of variation used in the identification strategy described in SectionIV.
The U.K. market has been at the forefront of policies to regulate both lenders and households' leverage. On the lender side, since the 1990s the U.K. microprudential regulator—the Financial Services Authority—has imposed time-varying, bank-specific minimum capital requirements on the banks under its purview (Aiyar et al. (2014)). In addition, since 2008 two approaches to calculate risk weights have coexisted: the standardized approach and the IRB approach. Under IRB, banks' own internal models are used to estimate risk components such as probabilities of default and loss given default, which are then used as inputs in the risk-weight functions defined by regulators. The six largest lenders in the United Kingdom have all adopted IRB models since 2008 when the capital regulation changed from Basel I to Basel II. In contrast, with very few exceptions, medium and small lenders have opted for the standardized approach, mostly because of the large fixed compliance cost associated with IRB models (Competition and Markets Authority (2015), Benetton et al. (2020)).
As a result, a two-tier system has prevailed, with large variation in risk weights for potentiallysimilarmortgage products.15Figure2shows risk weights for U.K. lenders in 2015 as a function of LTV. For lenders adopting the standardized approach, risk weights are fixed at 35% for LTVs up to 80%, and they increase to 45% on incremental balances above 80%. In contrast, lenders adopting an IRB model have risk weights that increase with LTV along the entire distribution. The average gap between the average IRB risk weight and the risk weight under the standardized approach is about 30 percentage points for mortgages with an LTV below 50%, compared to less than 15 percentage points for mortgages with leverage above 80%.
An alternative way to regulate leverage in the mortgage market is to impose explicit limits on how much households can borrow. Among recent examples of such policies are maximum debt-to-income (DTI) limits in the United States (DeFusco, Johnson, and Mondragon (2020)), and both LTI and LTV limits in Ireland (Acharya et al. (2020)). In the United Kingdom, in June 2014, the Financial Policy Committee recommended that regulated lenders limit mortgage originations with an LTI above 4.5% to 15% of the total number of new mortgage loans (Bank of England (2014)).16
In SectionIIof theInternet Appendix, I provide evidence on the effect of the policy on mortgage originations in a difference-in-difference setting, exploiting variation before and after the policy and across lenders in the fraction of mortgages with an LTI higher than 4.5 before the policy. I find that more affected lenders reduce their fraction of high-LTI mortgages by almost 4% more relative to other lenders, with the effect stronger in the market for first-time buyers.
In practice, both risk-weighted capital requirements for lenders and maximum LTV or LTI for households are complementary tools to regulate the mortgage market. In this paper, I provide a framework to study borrower- and lender-based macroprudential policies and their interactions, which can have important equilibrium effects. Most notably, in the counterfactual analysis of SectionVI, I explore two types of regulations: risk-weighted capital requirements for lenders and maximum LTV limits for borrowers.
Do differences in leverage regulation affect mortgage pricing? In this section, I discuss evidence on the relation between risk-weighted capital requirements and mortgage rates.17First, in Figure3, I explore the variation in rates across LTVs and lenders. I plot the mean predicted interest rate as a function of the LTV obtained from a regression of loan-level interest rates on a step function with steps at each 0.5 percentage point LTV bin and a rich set of mortgage, lender, and macro controls. I focus on a representative large lender using the IRB model and a representative small lender using the standardized approach. Both lenders set the interest rate as an increasing schedule of the LTV, which captures default risk (Schwartz and Torous (1989), Campbell and Cocco (2015)), with discrete jumps at certain maximum LTV thresholds as already documented by Best et al. (2020).18The rate schedule of the large lender shows clear discontinuous jumps at maximum LTVs, while the small lender increases sharply the rate only for LTVs above 80%, when risk weights start to increase as shown in Figure2. The large lender offers a more competitive interest for low-LTV mortgages. The gap in prices closes for intermediate-LTV mortgages and even reverses for products with an LTV above 85%, consistent with the shrinking gap in risk weights between internal models and the standardized approach.
Second, I further explore the relation between regulation and pricing by exploitingwithinlender variation in risk weights and interest rates over time. One concern with the pattern displayed in Figure3is that there are unobservable factors varying jointly across lenders and LTVs that affect pricing, and could also be correlated with risk weights. To mitigate this concern, I exploit the approval of an IRB model for a medium-sized lender and compare its risk weights and mortgage rates to those of another medium-sized lender that continues to adopt the standardized approach.
Figure4plots the average risk-weight and the average interest rate for the two medium-sized lenders for mortgages with a maximum LTV of 95% relative to those with a maximum LTV of 70%.19Panel (A) shows a large increase for the lender adopting the IRB model in the risk-weight of high-LTV mortgages, which makes the relative risk-weight jump from about 1.5 to more than 4. Panel (B) shows that the average interest rate on high-LTV mortgages relative to low-LTV mortgages increases from about 1.1 to about 1.6. At the same time, the lender that continues to adopt the standardized approach shows no change in relative pricing.
I next provide evidence on equilibrium mortgage originations across LTVs and lenders, which are the two main dimensions of variation in leverage regulation. Figure5, Panel (A), shows the LTV choice of first-time buyers. The vast majority of first-time buyers are concentrated at high maximum LTVs, with more than 25% borrowing (almost) exactly 90% of the value of their house.20This bunching behavior allows me to model the leverage choice as a discrete choice of the LTV band. Figure5, Panel (B), presents the quantity counterpart of the analysis of prices in Figure3. Most notably, I compute the portfolio shares of a representative large lender using an IRB model and a representative small lender using the standardized approach. The large-lender portfolio is evenly distributed across all leverage levels, while the small lender issues most mortgages at high LTVs, which is the segment in which its relative disadvantage is lower.
Overall, the evidence so far suggests that differences in leverage regulations across lenders and products can affect mortgage pricing and originations. The ultimate impact on market structure, however, may also depend on other factors that differ across lenders, such as incumbent advantage and brand recognition, which can affect borrower elasticities to the interest rate and substitution patterns following changes in regulation. I explore some of these factors further in Figure6.
Figure6, Panel (A), plots the price and market share of two mortgage products with the same maximum LTV (60%), interest rate type (two years fixed), and loan size (£140,000 to 160,000) but that are offered by two different lenders. I find that the mortgage with the higher price has the higher market share for the full period under analysis. In my model, I account for factors (e.g., brand value) that can help explain this counterintuitive effect and I further account for the role of lenders' branch network in borrowers' choices.21Figure6, Panel (B), shows the correlation for the six largest lenders between the branch share and the mortgage share in each postcode area. I find a strong positive relationship between local branch presence and mortgage originations.22Accounting for brand value and the branch network in the demand model is important to capture factors that can affect the demand elasticity (e.g., limited substitution due to local shopping), as the distance between the borrower and the lender continues to play an important role even in modern lending markets (Becker (2007), Scharfstein and Sunderam (2016), Nguyen (2019)).
My last set of motivating empirical facts relates to mortgage performance across LTVs and lenders. I study two main risks in the mortgage markets: default and refinancing. For each product, I capture default as the fraction of mortgages originated since 2005 that are in arrears in 2016 out of the total number of mortgages on lenders' balance sheet, and I capture refinancing risk as the fraction of outstanding mortgages in 2016 that are on an SVR out of the total number of mortgages on the lenders' balance sheet. The refinancing variable is defined as one minus the share paying the SVR, which is the reset rate that borrowers pay at the end of the initial fixed or discounted period.
Figure7shows default and refinancing patterns, looking at the joint variation across LTVs and across lenders using different models to compute risk weights.23In Panel A, the fraction of mortgages in arrears increases monotonically with the maximum LTV, from 0.8% for mortgages with an LTV below 70%, to more than 3% for mortgages with an LTV above 90%. This pattern is reflected in the pricing schedules of Figure3.24Interestingly, I do not find significant differences between large lenders using IRB models and medium small lenders using the standardized approach. In Panel B, I find a slight decrease in refinancing for high-LTV mortgages, but as before no differential patterns between lenders following different approaches to compute risk weights.
The absence of large differences in ex-post performance by LTV across lenders using different models to compute risk weights suggests that the large gaps in risk weights depicted in Figure2may be dye at least in part to differences in regulatory regime and in bank practices, rather than to intrinsic differences in actual risk.25This comparison is suggestive, given that I only observe risk weights, ex-ante characteristics, and ex-post outcomes, as the exact internal models are proprietary. However, the existence of a possible mismatch between risk weights and realized risk or expected risk as proxied by ex-ante characteristics is consistent with previous evidence (Aiyar et al. (2014), Behn, Haselmann, and Vig (2016b), Plosser and Santos (2018)).26The comparison between risk weights and ex-post performance is especially relevant for low-LTV mortgages, where arrears are less likely and loss-given-default is lower as a result of the lower initial leverage. A conservative regulator that sets risk weights for the standardized approach together with large banks trying to economize on capital may generate a large gap in risk weights for mortgages that do not exhibit large differences in ex-post performance.
article.III. A Structural Model of the U.K. Mort:
In this section, I develop a structural model of mortgage demand and pricing to study the equilibrium implications of changes in leverage regulation. I begin by specifying household utility as a function of mortgage characteristics and deriving both product and loan demand. I then develop a pricing equation that incorporates the empirical facts described in SectionII.
In each marketmand quartert,Imtheterogeneous borrowers, indexed byi, choose a mortgage to buy a house. I define a marketmas a combination of geographic region and demographic characteristics.27Borrowers simultaneously choose a mortgage product indexed byj, which is defined as a lender, interest rate type, and maximum LTV triplet, and a loan amount, given their preferences and budget constraint. I follow the characteristics approach (Lancaster (1979)) and assume that (i) each mortgage can be represented as a bundle of attributes and (ii) borrowers have preferences over these attributes.
I construct the choice set comparing borrowers with similar observable characteristics in the same market and quarter, and I impose two additional restrictions based on affordability and liquidity constraints. First, households may not be able to borrow up to the desired leverage due to supply side restrictions (such as LTV or LTI limits). Second, liquidity constraints may limit the ability of the household to increase their downpayment and consider products with lower maximum leverage. Both types of constraints restrict the choice set of households in terms of the maximum LTV among the full set available in the market.
The demand model jointly described by equations (2) and (3) captures borrowers' discrete product demand and continuous loan demand, respectively, in a general and flexible way. In practice, the model described so far implies only one exclusion restriction: branches enter the discrete product choice (equation (2)) but not the continuous quantity choice (equation (3)).30
Branches as well as other product characteristics inXj, such as the interest type and brand, allow for horizontal differentiation, which can lead to imperfect substitution across mortgages as a result of changes in relative interest rates. For example, higher branch presence can increase household utility because they lead to spatial differentiation. A large branch presence allows the household to walk into a branch when needed, lowering transaction costs. More branches can also make the lender more salient to the borrower by increasing the probability that the borrower will consider it. Moreover, in the absence of data on borrower assets, the local share of branches can proxy for preexisting relations between the borrower and the lender (e.g., current account).31
In the model, the interest raterjmtdoes not vary across individuali, in line with the lack of individual-based pricing discussed in SectionI.A, but does vary across all characteristics defining productsj, consistent, for example, with the pricing schedule across maximum LTVs and across lenders in Figure3. The sensitivity to the interest rate is driven jointly by the discrete-continuous choice and varies based on differences in borrowers' characteristics and preferences both across and within markets as well as on differences in product characteristics.32
Finally, I allow unobservable product heterogeneity(ξjmt)to affect the household mortgage and quantity choice. In the case of mortgage products, the unobservable term can capture “quality” as in standard industrial organization models (Berry, Levinsohn, and Pakes (1995), Nevo (2001)). Even if I observe the most relevant mortgage characteristics, lenders can offer optional services and contracts can include additional features (e.g., cash back, payment holidays) that may increase the “quality” of the product, ceteris paribus. Additionally, advertising plays an important role in the mortgage industry and could further justify the inclusion of unobservable product heterogeneity (Goeree (2008), Gurun, Matvos, and Seru (2016)).33
Equation (4) does not account for the two key risks in the mortgage market that I discuss in SectionII, namely, default and refinancing. First, default risk raises the lender's expected cost of issuing a mortgage. I assume that lenders setting interest rates do not forecast the probability of default in each period, but consider an average expected probability of default as in Crawford, Pavanini, and Schivardi (2018). Second, given the high level of refinancing at the end of the initial period, it is unreasonable to assume that lenders compute the present value as if all mortgages are held until maturity. I assume that lenders expect borrowers to refinance at the end of the initial period.34
Note that if there is no default risk (∂dijmt∂rjt=0anddijmt=0), all lenders offer only one product, and all households make only the discrete product choice(qijmt=1), then equation (8) collapses to the standard markup pricing formula:rjt∗=cjt−1∑m∑i∂sijmt∂rjt1sijmt.
Equation (8) characterizes the unconstrained optimal interest rate for lenders, but in reality, lenders set rates accounting for regulatory constraints in place. In the model, I focus on the two leverage regulations for the United Kingdom that I discuss in SectionII.A. First, I add a risk-weighted capital constraint to the bank optimization problem. Even if lenders' balance sheets have assets other than mortgages, I assume that when they set rates for mortgages, they account for the capital requirement constraint. Second, I embed regulation on household leverage in the model by imposing a quota on the share of mortgages with an LTI above 4.5.
The equilibrium in the mortgage market is characterized by both lenders' optimal pricing subject to leverage regulations and borrowers' optimal mortgage choice taking as given prices and other product characteristics.37
article.IV. Estimation and Identification:
In this section, I discuss how I take the model to the data. First, I describe how I build borrowers' choice sets, which are not observable in the data and could be affected by affordability criteria. Next, I discuss the estimation approach and the variation that I use for identification, endogeneity concerns, and supply-side instruments.
I determine the products available in borrowers' choice set in three steps. First, I break the population into markets defined by four demographic groups based on below- and above-median age and income jointly with the region in which the borrower's house is located. I assume that borrowers in each market can access all products sold in that market in the same quarter in which they borrow, but not those sold in other markets or other quarters. This restriction comes from the fact that borrowers with similar observable characteristics have access to similar alternatives. Restricting the choice set using some borrowers' demographics could also partially compensate for the absence of data on mortgage applications and on banks' loan approval decisions.38
Given the national nature of the market, I do not impose restrictions on the choice set based on locations finer that the level of the region. My analysis focuses on the largest lenders, with portfolios of mortgages from across the United Kingdom, and even products from smaller lenders, with a more local business model, can be sold nationally via Internet, the phone, and brokers. Ruling out products from households' choice sets based on their within-region location seems to be somewhat extreme and unrealistic in a market such as the United Kingdom. However, I allow for a narrower geographical level to play a role through the local branch network of the lender.
Second, in each market-quarter, I focus on the most popular mortgages offered by the 13 largest lenders, which account for more than 90% of mortgage originations in the period I analyze. The U.K. mortgage market has historically been very innovative, with a large number of products offered (Cocco (2013)). However, a lot of these products have very small market shares. For example, while mortgages are available with an initial fixed-rate period of 1 to 10 years, mortgages with a two-year fixed period account for more than 60% of originations in my sample. Accordingly, I group mortgages offered by lenders outside the 13 largest lenders and mortgages with a market share below 0.3% in a representative “outside” product.39
Finally, I implement an additional step within market-quarter to further restrict borrowers' choice set. A drawback of the approach to construct the choice set so far is that I can include products that are not in borrowers' choice sets, which can lead to biased parameter estimates (Goeree (2008), Gaynor, Propper, and Seiler (2016)). For example, maximum LTI and LTV limits can impose an upper bound on borrowers' leverage, while unobservable differences in wealth can act as a lower bound. In the data, I observe borrowers in the same market and quarter shopping at very different maximum LTV bands. Thus, I further restrict borroweri's choice set to only those mortgages with a maximum LTV equal to the one chosen byi, which I observe, and just above and below, which I observe for borrowers in the same market and quarter of borroweri.40Thus, I allow borrowers to shop locally in terms of the downpayment decision, consistent with the bunching behavior in Figure5.
I estimate the demand model in two steps. In the first step, I estimate simultaneously the discrete-continuous choice by maximizing the joint log-likelihood function for each market summing across all borrowers and quarters, that is,Lm=∑t∑iln(Li). The joint likelihood addresses the simultaneity bias that arises if I do not account for the discrete product choice (equation (12)) when I estimate the continuous quantity choice (equation (13)). I also account for possible correlation between the interest raterjmtand unobservable product characteristicsξjmtby including a full set of product-market-quarter fixed effectsδjmtin the log-likelihood function. Thus, in the first step, I obtain the utility parametersϕm,ηm,λm, the scaling factorsσmandμm, and the product-market-quarter fixed effectsδjmt.
In SectionIV.C, I discuss the supply-side instruments that I use to obtain consistent estimates of the parameters in equation (16).
It is important to note that equation (17) estimates the effect of variables at origination on ex-post outcomes. As some variables will change over time (e.g., income, house value), a fully specified model should control for the actual value of these variables. Unfortunately, I do not have a panel that will allow me to do that. However, variables at origination play an important role in pricing theexpectedprobability of arrears, which is the object of interest in the pricing function.41
Estimation of the demand parameters faces two endogeneity concerns arising from the simultaneity problem and from unobservable attributes affecting demand. First, I address the simultaneity problem by estimating the discrete-continuous choice jointly, as shown in equation (15). This approach allows me to address the bias that can arise if I do not account for the discrete product choice when I estimate the continuous quantity choice. To separately identify the discrete and continuous choices, I assume that lenders' local branch presence affects borrowers' choice among lenders, but not the conditional choice of quantity. Since I estimate the model in each region separately and I control for lender fixed effects, my assumption requires that within a region, a larger branch presence in a postcode area does not differentially affect the loan demand of borrowers choosing that lender.42I exploit variation in the branch network together with variation in the location of the borrowers' houses at the postcode level to identify the effect of branches on product demand.
Second, lenders endogenously choose interest rates, which could be correlated with unobservable attributes that also affect demand (e.g., advertising, screening, cash back). As an example, consider a lender that relaxes screening or offers cash back for a specific product and at the same time increases the interest rate on that product. Screening efforts and the cash-back option are not observable to the econometrician, and thus enter the error term(ξjmt), and at the same time are correlated with the interest raterjmt. As a result, I can observe borrowers choosing the product and mistakenly conclude that they are not responding to the higher interest rate, while the effect of the higher rate is countervailed by the differential screening effort or the cash-back option.
The endogenous interest raterjtis instrumented in the baseline specification with the lender risk weightsρjt, and I also estimate a specification with lender risk-weighted capital requirementsK̲ltρjt. The minimum capital requirementsK̲ltvary across lenders and over time at the quarterly frequency; while the risk weightsρjtvary across lenders, across LTV bands, and over time at the yearly frequency. Thus, the identifying variation for the endogenous interest rate is along three dimensions: (i) controlling for LTV and time, across lender because of different risk weights and minimum capital requirements; (ii) controlling for lender and time, across LTV bands because of different risk weights; and (iii) controlling for lender and LTV, over time because of changes in risk weights for lenders with IRB model and changes in minimum capital requirements for all lenders.
Equation (19) indicates that predetermined variation in risk weights (risk-weighted capital requirements) is uncorrelated with demand shocks, conditional on a rich set of macro-, lender-, and product-level controls. The intuition for this identifying assumption is that mortgage borrowers care about risk-weighted capital requirements only insofar as they affect mortgage rates. Endogeneity in the regulatory instrumentZjtthat is correlated with unobservable household preferencesξjmtcan pose a threat to my identification strategy. Institutional features, additional evidence, and robustness tests mitigate this concern.
First, my identification assumption is conditional on mortgage product characteristics, which include lender fixed effects and controls for high-LTV mortgages, thus requiring adifferentialchange in risk weights across LTVswithinlender, as an average change will be captured by the fixed effects. Furthermore, changes in the internal models need to be approved by the regulators, limiting lenders' discretion in setting them.43Second, the reduced-form evidence in SectionII.Dshows that lenders using internal models do not appear to be different from lenders adopting the standardized approach across LTV bands in both ex-ante borrower characteristics (age and income) and ex-post performance (arrears and refinancing). Third, I test the robustness of my demand estimates by instrumenting endogenous mortgage rates with the risk weights ofotherlenders.
While the key endogenous variables in my setting are mortgage rates, I briefly discuss the variation that allows me to identify other demand parameters. The covariation between the exogenous product characteristicsXjand borrowers' choices identifies the other parametersβmin equation (16). The product-market-time fixed effectsδjmtare identified in the first step from variation in market shares across products and over time, and correlation between the loan amount and the product choice in a market-quarter.44The scaling factorμmis identified by the different weight of the product-market-time fixed effectsδjmton the discrete relative to the continuous choice, while the scaling factorσmis identified by individual-level variation in both product and quantity choice within market that is not explained by other demographics. Correlation between the continuous loan amount and income within product-market-time across borrowers identifies the elasticity of loan demand to incomeϕm. Finally, the parameterηm, which captures the effects of demographics such as age and local house prices, is identified by both variation in loan demand within product-market-time and the correlation between borrowers' product choice and the interaction of product characteristics with borrowers' demographics.
Identification of the supply-side parametersτZcandτXcin equation (18) comes from correlation between cost shiftersZjtand product characteristicsXjwith marginal costsmcjtat the product level. The main cost shifters of interest for my analysis are risk-weighted capital requirements. As discussed in SectionII.A, capital requirements vary both across lenders and over time, due to regulatory changes, and across products offered by the same lender, due to the risk-weights adjustment. Most notably, minimum capital requirementsK̲ltvary across lenders and over time at the quarterly frequency, while risk weightsρjtvary across lenders, across LTV bands, and over time at the yearly frequency. Therefore, I identify the shadow cost of risk-weighted capital regulation controlling for (i) differences across lenders that are common among products (lender shocks) and (ii) differences across products that are common across lenders (market shocks).
I include two additional variables inZjtthat can affect marginal costs. First, a measure of the cost of lenders' wholesale funding based on secondary-market senior unsecured bond spreads. This measure is constructed by the Bank of England to track developments in lenders' funding cost and vary over time and across products based on the lender originating the mortgage. Second, I add the euro interest rates swaps for two and five years. Swap rates are a hedging instrument that lenders use when selling mortgages with fixed periods of two and five years, and thus, they vary over time and across products based on the initial fixed period.
I also allow the effect of risk-weighted capital requirements to vary across lenders. Heterogeneity in the effect of risk-weighted capital requirements is consistent with both my model and previous literature (Gambacorta and Mistrulli (2004), Kashyap, Stein, and Hanson (2010), Aiyar et al. (2014), Jiménez et al. (2017)).45Hence, I estimate equation (18) with an additional term obtained interacting risk-weighted capital requirements with a dummy for lenders with a capital buffer (wholesale funding cost) above the median.
I perform two tests to check the robustness of my estimates of the effect of risk-weighted capital requirements on product-level marginal costs. First, I estimate a version of equation (18) with interacted lender-quarter fixed effects. This specification allows me to identify the effect of risk-weighted capital requirements using variation within lender-quarter jointly across products with different LTV bands and over time due to changes in risk weights. This robustness check highlights the advantage of modeling lenders as multiproduct firms, as it allows me to control for unobservable confounding factors that vary jointly over time and across lenders.46
Second, to reduce concerns about endogeneity in risk weights for lenders using IRB models that could be correlated with omitted cost determinantsκjtc, I also estimate equation (18) using an instrumental variable strategy. Specifically, I exploit exogenous changes in capital requirementsK̲ltthat take place at the end of 2015 for some lenders and compare the evolution of their marginal costs to other lenders who did not experience changes in capital requirements at the same time.
Finally, similar to the demand side, I also estimate the impact of exogenous product characteristics on marginal costs captured by the parameterτXcin equation(18). The identification relies on how characteristics such as the length of the initial period, which captures interest rate risk, and maximum LTV, which captures default risk, covary with marginal costs.
article.V. Estimation Results:
In this section, I present results from estimation of the structural model using data for first-time buyers. TableIIshows the estimated demand parameters averaged across all markets.47The main parameter of interest isα, which captures the effect of the interest rate on indirect utility. As expected, I find that borrowers dislike more expensive mortgages and that the effect of mortgage rates on demand is highly significant. The point estimates across markets is approximately 0.025.
To understand the magnitude, I compute the discrete and continuous elasticities using the formulas reported in SectionIIIof theInternet Appendix. I find an average loan demand elasticity of about 0.07 and a product demand elasticity of 5.9 across all mortgage products. The loan demand elasticity implies that a 1-percentage-point increase in the interest rate leads to a 2.5% change in loan demand. This number is in line with the estimates from previous studies using very different approaches, such as bunching techniques (DeFusco and Paciorek (2017)) and survey data (Fuster and Zafar (2021)).48
The product demand elasticity implies that, on average, a 1% increase in the interest rate decreases the market share of the mortgage by almost 6%. When I focus on the 10 most popular products, I find that a 1% increase in the interest rate decreases the market share of the mortgage by 3% to 7%, while the shares of other mortgages increase by 0.01% to 0.07%. These own- and cross-price elasticities are in line with results in recent structural work looking at the U.K. and the U.S. mortgage markets (Buchak et al. (2018a), Robles-Garcia (2019)) and the Italian corporate loan market (Crawford, Pavanini, and Schivardi (2018)).49
Given that my product definition combines several elements of horizontal differentiation, I can compute elasticities at various levels. In TableIII, I report the average loan demand and own-product demand elasticity across mortgage characteristics. The six largest lenders and building societies have similar loan demand elasticity, while challenger banks face higher demand elasticity. In terms of product demand, building societies have the lowest elasticity, followed by the six largest lenders. Challenger banks face the highest elasticities of mortgage demand. As a result, for the same percentage point increase in interest rate, challenger banks lose more customers and face a larger decrease in loan demand from customers who buy their products. I also explore heterogeneity across leverage levels. Both loan and product demand elasticities increase with leverage. Mortgages with a maximum LTV above 85% have, on average, a loan demand elasticity of 0.09 and a product demand elasticity of 7.5 relative to mortgages with leverage below 70%, whose elasticities are 0.06 and 4.8, respectively.
TableIIalso reports the estimated demand parameters capturing preferences for other product characteristics, such as maximum leverage and the length of the fixed period, which play a central role in mortgage choice (Campbell and Cocco (2003), Badarinza, Campbell, and Ramadorai (2017)). I find that first-time buyers value mortgages with high leverage, which allow for lower downpayments for credit-constrained borrowers. I also find that borrowers prefer longer fixed-rate periods, as such contracts minimize switching costs involved in refinancing as well as interest rate risk. The fraction of branches in the postcode in which households have their houses has a positive but only marginally significant impact on average.
Finally, while so far I focus on average effects, the estimated model allows for rich heterogeneity, both observable and unobservable. TableIIreports the effect of individual income (ϕm) and unobservable heterogeneity (σm). I find a positive and significant elasticity of loan demand to income with a magnitude of 0.7. I also find significant unobservable heterogeneity across households, even within my narrowly defined market and after controlling for observable demographics within market.50
In SectionIVof theInternet Appendix, I look at the ability of the model to predict key variables of interest on the demand side, namely, loan demand, LTI, LTV and product market shares. TableIA.VIshows key moments predicted by the model and the corresponding moments in the data. Overall, the model fits the data well, in terms of both mean and variance. The main limitation is that the model underpredicts the variance in LTV shares, as it is not able to capture well the extreme leverage levels that are sometimes observed in the data.51TableIA.VIalso reports the out-of-sample fit of the model. Specifically, after estimating the structural demand parameters using data for 2015 and 2016, I predict loan demand, LTI, market shares, and LTV for mortgages in the first two quarters of 2017. Out of the estimation sample, the model captures the data well.
In SectionVof theInternet Appendix, I discuss several robustness checks. First, I instrument the endogenous interest rate for first-time buyers with both the risk-weighted capital requirements, thus exploiting additional variation in capital requirements over time and across lenders, and the risk weights for the same maximum LTV of other lenders. Second, I construct an initial annual percentage rate (APR) as a function of both the interest rate and the lender fee for a representative loan amount and use it as the price of the mortgage instead of the initial rate only. Third, I estimate the second step of the demand model (equation (16)) simultaneously with the supply side (equation (18)) using generalized method of moments. The demand estimates are robust to these different instruments, variable definitions, and estimation methods.
In this section, I present results from the estimation of the supply-side parameters. First, I discuss estimates of the default model given by equation (17), whose parameters enter the calculation of markups and effective marginal costs. TableIVreports the results. Column (1) uses the full cross-sectional data on arrears. I find a statistically significant positive relation between the interest rate and default. In terms of magnitude, my estimates imply that a 1-percentage-point higher interest rate increases the probability of default by 0.25 percentage points. Given an average default rate of about 1.3%, a 1-percentage-point increase in rate generates approximately a 20% increase in arrears.
I employ the estimates in column (1) in the baseline simulation, but I also explore alternative specifications for robustness. First, in columns (2) and (3) of TableIV, I split my sample into mortgages originated before and after the crisis. The relation between interest rate and default is positive and significant in both periods. The point estimate is higher before the crisis. For mortgages originated before the crisis, a 1-percentage-point higher interest rate increases the probability of default by 0.8 percentage points, while the effect is approximately five times smaller for mortgages issued after the crisis. Note, however, that average default is about 6% for mortgages originated before the crisis and only 1% for mortgages originated after the crisis. Thus, the effect of a 1-percentage-point higher interest rate relative to the mean level of arrears is approximately 14% in both sub-samples.
Second, in column (4) of TableIV, I address endogeneity concerns coming from omitted variables correlated with both the interest rate and the default probability. Specifically, I instrument the interest rate in equation (17) with supply-side cost shifters (i.e., risk weights and swap rates). I focus on mortgages originated after the crisis given that I do not have variation in risk-weights precrisis. The instrumental variable estimate of the marginal effect of mortgage rates on default is slightly stronger than the OLS estimate in column (3). A 1-percentage-point higher interest rate increases the probability of default by about 0.2 percentage points. The magnitude is in line with the point estimate using the full sample in column (1) of TableIV.
Third, the magnitudes that I find are in line with the estimates in previous studies that explore the effect of marginal changes in loan rate on firms' default (Crawford, Pavanini, and Schivardi (2018)), and use quasi-natural variation in mortgage rates to understand borrower default (Fuster and Willen (2017), Gupta (2019)). The most comparable estimates are by Fuster and Willen (2017), who find that a 2-percentage-point reduction in the interest rate decreases the probability of default by about 40%. In my setting, I find that a 2-percentage-point increase in rate leads to an increase in the probability of default of about 38%.52While my estimates are in line with previous work, in the counterfactual analysis in SectionVI, I also test the robustness of my results to doubling the effect of mortgage rates on default.
Finally, in TableIV, I also study how LTI and LTV at origination affect the probability of default. These two variables affect counterfactual default and mortgage rates as they endogenously respond in equilibrium to changes in mortgage rates via both the discrete product choice, which determines the LTV band, and the continuous loan choice, which for a given income determines the LTI. Column (1) shows that mortgages with LTI above 3.5 are significantly more likely to default, as expected, while mortgages with an LTV above 85% are less likely to default, which is perhaps surprising. The analysis by mortgages originated before and after the crisis helps explain this pattern. Controlling for the interest rate, high-LTV mortgages issued before the crisis are significantly more likely to default, while high-LTV mortgages issued after are significantly less likely to default. A possible explanation for the latter result may be the increase in supply-side restrictions and affordability checks after the crisis, which led to an overall low volume of originations at high leverage to a select pool of low-risk borrowers.
Using lenders' first-order conditions and the estimated demand and default parameters, I compute product-level markups and marginal costs. The average markup is about 0.50 percentage points in the full sample, which corresponds to about 18% of the average interest rate in the data, and the average marginal cost is about 2.40%.53
I next use the estimated marginal costs as a dependent variable in equation (18) to identify the cost of capital regulation in the mortgage market, controlling for product characteristics and other supply-side factors. TableVshows the structural supply parameters. The main parameter of interest captures the effect of risk-weighted capital requirements on the marginal costs. I identify this effect by exploiting variation in risk-weighted capital requirements across lenders, across leverage levels, and over time. Column (1) of TableVshows the unconditional effect of capital regulation on the marginal cost. I find that a 1-percentage-point higher risk-weighted capital requirement increases the marginal cost of lending to first-time buyers by about 20 basis points.
In column (2) of TableV, I control for product characteristics and other supply-side factors that affect the cost of issuing mortgages. The effect of risk-weighted capital requirements on lender marginal cost of originating mortgages is still significant. A 1-percentage-point higher risk-weighted capital requirement increases the marginal cost of lending to first-time buyers by approximately 26 basis points, controlling for differences across lenders, which are common among products (lender shocks), and for differences across products, which are common across lenders (macroshocks).
In column (3) of TableV, I report results after including interacted lender-quarter fixed effects. In this specification, I only exploit the variation in risk-weighted capital requirements within a lender-time pair, which rules out concerns about other time-varying lender-specific factors affecting the cost of issuing mortgages. The coefficient that captures the impact of regulation on the cost of lending is still significant and the magnitude is slightly larger than, but not statistically different from, that in column (2).
In columns (4) and (5) of TableV, I explore the heterogeneity in the cost of risk-weighted capital requirements across lenders. In column (4), I interact capital requirements with a dummy equal to 1 for lenders with a high capital buffer, defined as the difference between capital resources and capital requirements. In column (5), I interact capital requirements with a dummy equal to one for lenders with a high funding spread. I find that lenders with a higher buffer are less affected, while lenders with a higher funding spread are more affected, by the risk-weighted capital requirements. Both heterogeneity results are consistent with the literature on financial frictions, which suggests that capital constraints matter more for banks that are less capitalized in bad times, as proxied in my setting by a low buffer and a high funding spread (Gambacorta and Mistrulli (2004), Kashyap, Stein, and Hanson (2010), Aiyar et al. (2014), Jiménez et al. (2017)).
Finally, in column (6) of TableV, I report estimates of equation (18) using the instrumental variable strategy. I estimate a difference-in-difference specification in which I instrument risk-weighted capital requirements with a dummy equal to 1 after 2015 for lenders who experience an increase in capital requirements at the end of 2015. The instrumental variable specification yields point estimates for the effect of risk-weighted capital requirements on lenders' marginal costs of around 27 basis points. The effect is slightly larger, but not significantly different from the baseline estimates in column (2) of TableV.
While the coefficient capturing the effect of leverage regulation on marginal costs is the main supply-side parameter of interest, the coefficients on product characteristics and other supply-side cost shifters provide an important check on the plausibility of the cost estimates. As expected, I find that higher leverage and longer fixed-rate mortgages have higher marginal costs. Even controlling for the duration of the fixed period, larger time-varying swap rates have a positive and marginally significant effect on the cost of mortgage lending. Finally, higher funding spreads increase lenders' costs of originating mortgages, but the estimates are noisy.
The baseline estimate in column (2) of TableVshows an increase in the marginal cost of about 26 basis points for a 1-percentage-point increase in the risk-weighted capital requirement. Given an average marginal cost of 2.4%, marginal cost increases by approximately 11% for the average mortgage product.
To date there is no consensus on the impact of changes in capital requirements on the cost for lenders (Kovner and Van Tassel (2018)). Recent studies using different identification strategies and assumptions find estimates ranging from 3 basis points (Kisin and Manela (2016)), to 25 to 45 basis points (Hanson, Kashyap, and Stein (2011)), up until 60 to 90 basis points (Baker and Wurgler (2015)). To put my estimates of the cost of capital regulation into context, I simulate a 10-percentage-point increase in capital requirements, along the lines of previous studies (Hanson, Kashyap, and Stein (2011), Firestone, Lorenc, and Ranish (2017)).54
I find that increasing capital requirements by 10 percentage points increases marginal costs by about 60 basis points in the mortgage market. This effect lies on the upper end of previous estimates and can be interpreted as anupper boundon the cost of a common increase in capital requirements. First, my estimates are not directly comparable because I do not impose a particular capital structure model or exploit a single observed regulatory change keeping all else equal. Different from most previous studies on the topic, I infer theperceivedtotal cost of complying with the regulation only from the rates that lenders charge to mortgage borrowers after changes in risk-weighted capital requirements. Second, I allow lenders to adjust to the new regulationonlyalong one margin: repricing mortgages. In reality, lenders can lower deposit rates, issue new equity, and retain earnings, especially if the change in regulation is spread over time. These additional margins of adjustment likely decrease the cost of increasing capital requirements (Elliott (2009)).
article.VI. Counterfactual Leverage Regulations:
In this section, I use the estimated model to study alternative leverage regulations and their equilibrium impact on mortgage rates, market structure, and risk. First, SectionVI.Acompares two alternative counterfactuals that remove the regulatory-risk-weights gap between large and small lenders that I document SectionII.A. Specifically, I simulate a counterfactual in which all lenders calculate risk-weighted capital requirements using the standardized approach and an alternative counterfactual in which small and medium lenders also adopt a representative IRB model.
Second, SectionVI.Banalyzes the interactions between risk-weighted capital requirements and regulations limiting household leverage via LTVs. I impose a maximum LTV limit by removing from households' choice set all mortgages with leverage above 90% both in a counterfactual market with homogeneous capital requirements, as was the case before the financial crisis, and in the current regime with risk-weighted capital requirements.
The reduced-form evidence discussed in SectionIIsuggests that risk weights affect pricing and specialization. Here, I quantify the equilibrium effect of alternative counterfactual policies that address the regulatory-risk-weights gap between large banks and other competitors. First, I illustrate how current differences in capital regulation affect mortgage rates over and above other factors through the lens of the model.
The higher risk weights for the small lender translate into higher rates, ceteris paribus. If the elasticity of demand for products offered by large lenders is lower, due, for example, to brand power, there is also an incumbent advantage, which affects the price gap between large lenders and smaller competitors.
TableIA.VIIIin theInternet Appendixexplores the effect of a common increase in capital requirements(K̲lt)for all lenders. I find that after a common increase in capital requirements, large lenders using IRB models raise mortgage interest rates by about 50% less than small banks using the standardized regulatory approach. As a result of the differential impact, the largest lenders gain market share from their smaller competitors, increasing concentration in the mortgage market.
Here I explore the consequences of addressing the heterogeneity in regulations by changing how lenders compute risk weights(ρjt). Figure8illustrates two alternatives. First, I simulate an equilibrium without IRB models for the calculation of risk weights, which I label “All Standard.” Second, I allow lenders using a standardized approach to develop an internal model, using the average risk weights of large lenders. I label this second counterfactual case “All Internal.” TableVIreports the results for several variables of interest in a random subset of the first-time buyer market.56
Panel A shows the effects of removing heterogeneity in risk-weighted capital requirements on market structure. I measure concentration in the market looking at both the Herfindahl index (HI) and the share of the six largest lenders for mortgage originations. The abolition of internal models leads the market to become more competitive. Large lenders lose the regulatory advantage (first element in equation (20)) and increase their prices following an increase in the risk weights for mortgages they originate. As a result of the higher rates, large lenders lose market share to smaller lenders already adopting the standardized regulatory approach. The share of the six largest lenders drops by more than 20% from almost 85% to less than 70%. Small lenders' adoption of internal models also has a procompetitive effect on the market. I find that the HI declines from 16% to about 13%, as smaller lenders reduce prices and gain about 14 percentage points of market share, following the decrease in regulatory costs. The redistribution from large to small lenders is overall slightly less pronounced than in the first counterfactual.
Panel B of TableVIlooks at aggregate costs, prices, and demand.
