I find that eliminating IRB models increases costs in the market by about 53 basis points, which are passed onto borrowers via higher initial rates. The latter increases by approximately 53 basis points, or about 20% relative to the baseline rate. As a result, the Lerner index decreases by about 16%, from a baseline of about 20 to about 17. As a result of higher mortgage prices, product demand decreases by 14%, as almost 800 borrowers switch to the outside option. The average loan size decreases by approximately £1,600, which is slightly higher than 1% of the average baseline loan amount.
In the all-IRB model counterfactual, marginal costs in the mortgage market decrease by about 15 basis points, as a result of lower capital requirements for small lenders. This decline translates into a reduction in prices of about 15 basis points. Overall, in this scenario, the Lerner index increases by about 8%. The slightly lower rates yield an increase in mortgage demand of slightly more than 1%, while there is almost no difference in loan amounts.
I use the model to compute a measure of consumer surplus based on the sum of indirect utilities and lenders' profits.57Panel C of TableVIreports the results. When all banks adopt the standardized approach, as a result of overall higher prices, average consumer surplus decreases by more than 30%; while when all banks adopt an internal model, the lower prices increase consumer surplus by about 8%. Quarterly lenders' profit decrease by approximately £1.5 million following the adoption of the standard approach, or almost 15% relative to the baseline. As a result of the adoption of IRB models by medium and small lenders, overall profit increases by about 1.2%.
Even if a full evaluation of the policy from a systemic point of view would require a general equilibrium approach, the model can be used to learn the effects of changing capital regulation on risk in the mortgage market and its differential impact on large systemic lenders. In Panel D of TableVI, I first look at borrower default. The expected default predicted by the model in the baseline case is about 1.3%, which is in line with the data (see TableI). With the abolition of IRB models, I observe an increase in the average default in the mortgage market by approximately 8%, as higher prices make it harder for borrowers to service their monthly payments. In the second counterfactual, lower prices and relatively small changes in borrower demand translate into a lower default rate, which decreases by approximately 3%.
Finally, in Panel D of TableVI, I also report a measure of resilience for the overall mortgage market. I compute the equity buffer as the difference in pounds between lenders' equity and expected losses for each mortgage. Lenders' equity is given by the endogenous loan size multiplied by the lender-specific capital requirement and the counterfactual risk weights; expected losses come from expected default given by equation (17) after lenders reoptimize rates in reaction to the new risk-weighted capital requirements and borrowers adjust demand to the new prices. In the baseline the equity buffer is about £2.2, with a large difference between the six top lenders and other lenders. The largest lenders have a buffer of about £1.9, which is less than half the buffer of other lenders. This difference is driven by large gaps in risk weights and small differences in observed ex-post performance, consistent with the motivating facts discussed in SectionII.
Moving to the counterfactuals, I find that abolishing internal models doubles the equity buffer in the mortgage market, as large lenders experience an increase in their risk weights, especially for low-risk mortgages. In the all IRB model counterfactual, I find a small reduction in the extra buffer in the economy of about 4%, which is driven exclusively by small lenders, which experience a large drop in risk weights, especially for low-risk mortgages. However, the buffer of small lenders remains positive and higher than that of large lenders, which experience almost no change as a result of the policy. Given the central role played by large lenders in a crisis, my second counterfactual suggests that the reduction of small lenders' risk weights will not threaten the stability of the system (Acharya, Engle, and Richardson (2012), Akerlof et al. (2014), Bianchi (2016)). On the contrary, the procompetitive effect will decrease the intermediation conducted by large lenders, thus reducing their systemic importance.
To summarize, I find that heterogeneous capital regulation accounts for about 20% of the concentration in the market. The abolition of internal models addresses the imbalance between large and small lenders in terms of capital requirements, but the higher risk weights increase rates, reducing demand and consumer surplus. Extending a representative internal model to small lenders could also address the competitive distortion due to the differential regulatory treatment, with limited impact on consumer surplus, lender profits, ex-post mortgage defaults, and the resilience of large lenders.58
While the quantitative estimates cannot be applied outside the specific context I study, the insight that heterogeneity in leverage regulation affects competition in the mortgage market, with implications for borrower surplus, lender profits, and risk, goes beyond the U.K. mortgage market. In several countries in Continental Europe and in Australia where lenders' mortgage funding is deposit-based and there is no government insurance, it is likely that the forces I uncover for the U.K. mortgage market also hold.59For example, Behn, Haselmann, and Vig (2016b) find that in Germany, large banks using IRB models expanded lending to firms, potentially at the expense of smaller banks. In other countries such as the United States, the large recourse to securitization via GSEs and the emergence of shadow banks open up different margins of adjustment, which can impact the response of traditional banks to changes in risk-weighted capital regulation (Buchak et al. (2018a)).
An alternative way to regulate leverage in the mortgage market is to impose explicit limits on how much households can borrow. In this section, I use the estimated structural model to study the equilibrium effects of regulation that limits household leverage via LTV limits. Recent papers have explored similar policies, such as maximum DTI limits in the United States (DeFusco, Johnson, and Mondragon (2020)), and both LTI and LTV limits in Ireland (Acharya et al. (2020)). I contribute to this literature by providing a framework that allows one to study alternative policies under consideration. For example, the effects of imposing a maximum LTV limit at 80% relative to 90%. Specifically, I study the interaction of maximum LTV limits on borrowers and the capital requirement regime in place for lenders. In this way, I shed light on the equilibrium effects of jointly regulating both lenders' and borrowers' leverage.
Figure9shows the distribution of market shares by LTV in the baseline and after the elimination of mortgages with leverage above 90%. Panel (A) shows the equilibrium with a common capital requirement of 8% and a risk-weight of 50% for all lenders, as was the case before the global financial crisis during the Basel I regime, while Panel (B) shows the equilibrium with the actual risk-weighted capital requirements. As expected, mortgages with LTVs close to 90% experience the largest increase, but as prices adjust, other products are affected in equilibrium. TableVIIreports the quantification of costs and benefits and explores potential unintended consequences due to the interaction of multiple leverage regulations.
Panel A shows marginal costs, mortgage rates, and borrowers' demand. With homogeneous capital requirements at 8% and a 50% risk weight, both marginal costs and mortgage rates are higher than under the observed risk weighted capital requirements.60Mortgage rates are on average almost 3% with homogeneous capital regulations and about 2.6% in the current regime with risk-weighted requirements. As a result of eliminating high-leverage-high-cost mortgages, marginal costs go down and interest rates follow. The reduction in costs and interest rates is larger in the current regime with risk-weighted capital requirements, as high-leverage mortgages require more equity funding because of the higher risk weights. With homogeneous capital requirements, the decline in rates is about 4.5%, while with risk-weighted requirements, the decline is almost 8%. Despite the overall lower rates, there is a reduction in product demand, which drops between 1% and 4%.61
Panel B of TableVIIshows the effect on borrowers and lenders. The reduction in consumer surplus as a result of the policy is large despite the decrease in prices, which should increase it. The large negative effect is driven not only by borrowers switching to the outside option, but also by the positive valuation that households attach to high-leverage mortgages, as I show in TableII. Consumer surplus declines by about 12% and 5% in the homogeneous and risk-weighted capital regime, respectively. Lender profits drop by around 3.7% when capital requirement is homogeneous, while the decrease is 2% under risk-weighted capital requirements.
Finally, Panel C of TableVIIshows how regulating household leverage with LTV limits affects risk in the mortgage market. The limit to high-leverage mortgages decreases defaults, which drop by about 3% to 5% in both cases as a result of lower prices and lower leverage. To capture the overall risk in the mortgage market, I also look at lenders' equity buffers. In the counterfactual with homogeneous capital requirements, there is almost no change in the buffer or a slightly positive change, as lower prices reduce defaults. In the scenario with risk-weighted capital requirements, there is a 4% decrease in the equity buffer in the market as a result of the ban on mortgages with the highest risk but also the highest risk weights. According to my estimates, the decline in the buffer is even larger for the largest lenders, which experience a drop of more than 5%, thus increasing their exposure to risk in the mortgage market.
To summarize, I find that a regulation targeting LTV can be effective in reducing borrower defaults, but with large impacts for borrowers.62Furthermore, I show that the interaction in equilibrium between regulations on borrowers and lenders can have distributional effects and unintended consequences. The larger effects of abolishing high-LTV mortgages on consumer surplus and lender profits under homogeneous capital requirements are due to the fact that low-LTV borrowers are “subsidizing” high-LTV borrowers, while the opposite occurs under risk-weighted capital requirements, given the observed risk weights and defaults. Finally, leverage limits applied in a market with risk-weighted capital regulations can reduce the equity buffer of large lenders, thereby increasing systemic risk. This result emphasizes the importance of looking at equilibrium effects, especially when multiple regulations are in place and the implication of changing one may depend on other policies.
article.VII. Conclusion:
Leverage regulation has been at the center of the academic and policy debate since the global financial crisis, and there is an ongoing effort to better understand the channels through which it operates and to evaluate its effectiveness. In this paper, I focus on leverage regulation in the U.K. mortgage market, in which lenders face different capital regulations and limits to household leverage have been recently introduced. I develop a tractable equilibrium model of the mortgage market that accounts for several features characterizing borrower demand and lender competition, and I estimate the model using a new identification strategy that exploits variation in risk-weighted capital requirements both across lenders and within lender across mortgages with different LTVs.
I quantify the cost of capital regulation for lenders and find that a 1-percentage-point increase in risk-weighted capital requirements raises lenders' costs by 26 basis points or 11% for the average mortgage product when lenders can react only by adjusting their price. With the estimated model, I show that heterogeneous leverage regulation shapes market structure. In particular, removing the policy-driven difference in risk weights can promote competition without exacerbating risk. Finally, I find that LTV limits reduce borrower defaults but have a large impact on originations and consumer surplus, and can potentially reduce large lenders' risk-weighted equity buffers, and thereby affect systemic risk.
My paper can be extended in several directions. The lenders' problem can be expanded to account for the acceptance/rejection margin. This would allow leverage regulation to affect loan supply not only through changes in loan rates, but also through changes in underwriting standards. I capture this channel in a reduced-form way through affordability constraints, as well as unobservable product characteristics. A more comprehensive model and empirical strategy that feature both pricing and rejection choices would be an interesting avenue for future research. Moreover, in this paper, I focus mostly on the costs of risk-weighted capital requirements, their transmission to interest rates, and their implications for market structure. It would be interesting to enrich the framework to account explicitly for endogenous default choices on both the demand and the supply side. Adding the default option for borrowers would allow for a comprehensive measure of consumer surplus, while lenders' bankruptcy choices would provide an explicit microfoundation for leverage regulation and a fully fledged quantification of the trade-offs.
